20 min read
Just now

--

How can an AI write essays, solve coding problems, and answer questions like a human? Is it truly intelligent, or is there a surprisingly simple idea behind it? In this blog, we’ll explore Large Language Models intuitively, breaking down complex concepts into easy-to-understand explanations without overwhelming mathematics or technical jargon.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Prerequisites :
A curious mind to understand AI
So, Let’s go….
1. Why Are Large Language Models So Surprising?
Imagine traveling back to 2015 and telling a software engineer:
“In a few years, a computer will write essays, generate code, explain quantum physics, summarize books, translate languages, create business plans, and hold conversations that feel remarkably human.”
Most people would probably laugh.
Not because these tasks were impossible, but because they seemed to require something uniquely human: intelligence.
Yet today, millions of people interact daily with systems such as ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, and Llama. These systems can answer questions, solve problems, write stories, generate software, and even help researchers discover new ideas.
This naturally raises an important question:
How is this possible?
Did we somehow build a digital brain?
Did we finally create artificial intelligence?
Or is something else happening behind the scenes?
Before we dive into the technical details, let’s perform a small experiment.
Complete the following sentence:
The sun rises in the ______
Most people instantly think:
east
Now try this:
Fish live in the ______
You probably predicted:
water
Interesting, isn’t it?
You didn’t calculate these answers mathematically. You simply learned patterns from experience.
Throughout your life, you have encountered these phrases thousands of times. Your brain has become extremely good at predicting what comes next.
Now imagine a machine doing the same thing.
Not after reading a few books.
Not after reading a few million books.
But after reading a significant fraction of the internet.
That simple idea forms the foundation of modern Large Language Models (LLMs).
At their core, LLMs are prediction machines.
They are trained to predict the next piece of text given everything they have seen before.
Surprisingly, when this prediction process is scaled to enormous amounts of data and computing power, something remarkable happens.
Capabilities emerge.
The model begins to appear intelligent.
The rest of this article is the story of how that happens.
2. What Exactly Is a Language Model?
Let’s begin with a simpler question.
Imagine I ask you to complete this sentence:
The cat sat on the ______
What are some possible answers?
You might think of:

• mat

• chair

• floor

• couch
Notice something important.
There is not just one possible answer.
There are multiple possibilities, each with different likelihoods.
Your brain automatically assigns probabilities.
Perhaps:

• mat → 70%

• chair → 15%

• floor → 10%

• couch → 5%
You don’t consciously compute these numbers, but you intuitively know some answers are more likely than others.
A language model does exactly this.
Mathematically, it attempts to estimate:
Which simply means:
Given everything I have seen so far, what is the probability of each possible next word?
Let’s look at another example.
Suppose the model sees:
The capital of France is
Possible predictions:
Press enter or click to view image in full size

The model chooses from these probabilities and generates text one token at a time.
This is why language models are often described as giant autocomplete systems.
But that description is both correct and misleading.
Correct because they do predict what comes next.
Misleading because the scale is extraordinary.
Your phone’s autocomplete may know a few thousand words.
Modern LLMs learn patterns from trillions of words.
The difference is like comparing a bicycle to a spacecraft.
Both move.
One just happens to leave the atmosphere.
3. The Long Road to Modern LLMs
Large Language Models did not appear overnight.
They are the result of decades of research.
To understand why Transformers changed everything, we first need to understand what came before them.
Think of this section as a brief archaeological dig through the history of language modeling.
The Era of N-Grams
Early language models were surprisingly simple.
Suppose we want to predict the next word in:
I love machine ______
The system looks through a huge collection of text and asks:
What words historically appeared after “I love machine”?
Maybe it finds:

• learning

• tools

• intelligence
and chooses the most frequent option.
This approach is called an N-gram model.
It only looks at a small number of previous words.
While useful, it suffers from a major limitation.
Consider:
The professor who taught the class that inspired the students published a new ______
To predict the final word correctly, the model may need information from far earlier in the sentence.
N-grams quickly forget.
Their memory is tiny.
Recurrent Neural Networks (RNNs)
Researchers realized language requires memory.
Enter Recurrent Neural Networks.
Imagine reading a book while carrying a notebook.
Every time you read a sentence, you write down important information.
The notebook represents the hidden state of an RNN.
As the model reads each word, it updates this memory.
For the first time, language models could theoretically remember long sequences.
This was a major breakthrough.
Yet a problem remained.
The Forgetful Student Problem
Imagine trying to remember the first sentence of a 500-page novel while reading the final page.
Difficult, right?
RNNs faced a similar issue.
Information from earlier words gradually faded away.
This phenomenon became known as the vanishing gradient problem.
The model technically had memory.
But it often forgot important details before it could use them.
LSTMs: Smarter Memory
Researchers responded by building Long Short-Term Memory Networks (LSTMs).
Think of an LSTM as an upgraded notebook with intelligent filters.
Instead of recording everything, it learns:

• What to remember

• What to forget

• What to update
This dramatically improved performance.
For years, LSTMs dominated natural language processing.
Many believed they represented the future.
Then, in 2017, everything changed.
The Transformer Revolution
Researchers asked a radical question:
What if we stop reading words one by one?
Traditional models processed language sequentially.
Word after word.
Like a person reading through a keyhole.
Transformers introduced a different idea.
What if every word could immediately look at every other word?
Suddenly, information no longer needed to travel through hundreds of intermediate steps.
Any word could directly access relevant information anywhere in the sentence.
This mechanism became known as attention.
And attention would eventually power:

• ChatGPT

• Claude

• Gemini

• Llama
Modern AI was born from this insight.
But before we understand attention, we need to answer another question.
How does a machine even represent words in the first place?
4. Tokenization: Turning Language Into Building Blocks
Suppose I show a computer the word:
elephant
What does the computer actually see?
Not an elephant.
Not a word.
Not a meaning.
Just symbols.
Computers only understand numbers.
Therefore, before any learning can occur, language must be converted into a machine-friendly format.
This process is called tokenization.
What Is a Token?
A token is a small unit of text.
Sometimes a token is a whole word.
Sometimes it is only part of a word.
For example:
unbelievable
might become:

• un

• believe

• able
These pieces are tokens.
Similarly:
ChatGPT is amazing
might become:

• Chat

• GPT

• is

• amazing
Different models use different tokenization strategies.
Why Not Store Entire Words?
Imagine creating a vocabulary containing every possible word.
Immediately we encounter problems.
Consider:

• cybersecurity

• cyberattacks

• cybercrime

• cyberwarfare
Thousands of new words appear constantly.
Instead of memorizing every word, models learn reusable pieces.
This dramatically reduces complexity.
The LEGO Analogy
Think of language as a giant LEGO universe.
You could manufacture millions of complete structures.
Or you could manufacture a smaller collection of reusable blocks.
Modern tokenizers choose the second option.
A few thousand building blocks can construct millions of words.
This is more efficient.
More flexible.
And far more scalable.
Why Tokenization Matters
Many surprising behaviors of LLMs originate from tokenization.
For example:
A model does not see:
Artificial Intelligence
the way humans do.
It sees a sequence of tokens.
Everything that follows depends on those tokens.
Tokenization is therefore the first stage of transforming language into mathematics.
And mathematics is where the real magic begins.
5. Embeddings: Giving Words Coordinates in Meaning Space
Suppose I ask:
Which words are more similar?

• Cat and Dog

• Cat and Airplane
Humans answer instantly.
Cat and dog clearly share more meaning.
But how can a machine understand that?
The answer lies in embeddings.
From Words to Coordinates
Imagine a gigantic map.
Every word occupies a location on that map.
Words with similar meanings appear close together.
Words with unrelated meanings appear far apart.
Instead of representing words as text, we represent them as vectors.
For example:
Cat → [0.12, -0.55, 1.23, ...]
Dog → [0.10, -0.51, 1.18, ...]
Airplane → [4.21, -2.30, 7.90, ...]These numbers may look meaningless.
Yet together they encode relationships.
A City of Meanings
Imagine standing in a city where every building represents a concept.
Nearby buildings share similar meanings.
Faraway buildings represent very different ideas.
In this city:

• Doctor may be close to Nurse

• King may be close to Queen

• Paris may be close to France
The model learns these positions automatically from data.
Nobody manually places them there.
The relationships emerge during training.
The Famous King-Queen Example
One of the most famous discoveries in machine learning is:
At first glance this seems impossible.
How can simple numbers understand royalty and gender?
The answer is that embeddings capture patterns hidden within language.
The model repeatedly observes:

• kings are male rulers

• queens are female rulers
Eventually these relationships become encoded geometrically inside vector space.
Meaning becomes structure.
Language becomes mathematics.
Why Embeddings Changed AI
Embeddings transformed words from isolated symbols into connected concepts.
Before embeddings:
Words were treated like disconnected labels.
After embeddings:
Words became locations in a rich semantic universe.
This simple idea became one of the most important breakthroughs in modern AI.
In fact, everything that follows in this series, attention, transformers, reasoning, retrieval systems, and modern LLMs, depends on these vectors.
The journey has now transformed language into mathematics.
In the next section, we will explore the beating heart of modern AI:
Attention.
The mechanism that allows every word to decide what matters and what can be ignored.
6. Understanding Semantic Space
Before we understand attention, we must understand the world in which attention operates.
The world of vectors.
The world of semantic space.
A Strange New Universe
Imagine entering a gigantic universe where every word, phrase, and concept exists as a point floating in space.
Not physical space.
Meaning space.
In this universe:

• Dog is close to Puppy.

• Doctor is close to Surgeon.

• Paris is close to France.

• Basketball is close to Sports.
Meanwhile:

• Banana is far from Quantum Mechanics.

• Airplane is far from Happiness.
Distance represents similarity.
The closer two concepts are, the more related they tend to be.
The Library Thought Experiment
Imagine a magical library.
Every book ever written has been placed on shelves according to meaning rather t
